title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Colonel Barry, Miss Bustle, Mr. and Mrs. Gardner, Hercule Poirot, and the vicar, Stephen Lane, engage in a conversation regarding modern social habits and the nature of the seaside. Mrs. Gardner expresses concern regarding the casual nature of modern youth, specifically worrying that her daughter, Erin, might develop excessive body hair if she sunbathes too much. Poirot draws an indignation-inducing comparison, likening the sight of bodies lined up on the beach to a mortuary or meat on a butcher's slab.

After the Gardners depart, Stephen Lane joins the group. The discussion shifts to physical activities and physiological issues, such as seasickness and acrophobia. Miss Bustle mentions that Mrs. Redfern suffers from severe dizziness at heights. Christine Redfern then joins the group after swimming, noting that her pale skin does not tan but instead develops rashes. During the conversation, Miss Bustle suggests that Poirot play a joke on Mrs. Gardner by telling her a murderer is staying at the hotel, to which Poirot remarks that she would likely believe him.

The conversation turns to the possibility of crime in such a peaceful location. While Miss Bustle believes the area is too tranquil for murder, Poirot argues that evil exists everywhere, even under the sun. He explains that the seaside provides a perfect cover for enemies to meet, as the tradition of August vacations makes the presence of any individual appear natural and inconspicuous. Stephen Lane concludes the discussion by reflecting on Poirot's words, noting that the human heart is full of evil and madness.
